content.text:
Coverage is strong. This codebase has been hardened over multiple passes. My findings will be modest and focused on genuine, cheap-to-fix items. Let me verify the numpy resample memory finding is real and not already chunked, and confirm the two-set-pass in pipeline. Both confirmed from reads.

Let me do a final check on whether `descriptive_stats` and `_quantile` have any issue, and whether the `_resample_stability` numpy path could use `np.searchsorted` on sorted null for O((R+N)logN) instead of O(R·N). That's a legitimate algorithmic improvement but the pure path is also O(R·N), so it's parity. The memory blowup is the real issue.

Let me also verify there's no test for the unkeyed-trail warning and the `verify_trail=False` path (test-quality). 

I have enough. Let me compile findings.

findings.title: numpy resample-stability path allocates a full R×N boolean matrix (peak-memory blowup vs pure path)
findings.description: The numpy branch of `_resample_stability` computes `null_arr[None, :] >= res_arr[:, None]` (and the `<=` variant), materializing a dense R×N boolean array before `.sum(axis=1)`. This is O(R·N) PEAK MEMORY, whereas the pure-Python fallback it claims to merely 'speed up' is O(N) memory (one resample at a time). At corpus scale this inverts: e.g. R=10k resamples vs an N=10k permutation null is 10^8 booleans ≈ 100 MB transient just for the comparison mask, and large nulls (which whole-corpus permutation tests routinely use) make it worse. So enabling the optional `baseline`/numpy extra silently raises peak memory by orders of magnitude exactly on the inputs the vectorization is meant to help, and can OOM where the pure path would not. The docstring frames the numpy path as 'purely a speed optimization, never a semantic change' — but it is also a large, undocumented memory regression.
findings.poc:
import numpy as np
from assay_engine.methodology import confirm
# R=20000 resamples, N=20000 null -> a 20000x20000 bool mask = 4e8 bytes (~400MB) allocated
# transiently inside _resample_stability, vs the pure path's O(N) working set.
null = list(np.random.default_rng(0).normal(size=20000))
res  = list(np.random.default_rng(1).normal(0.5, size=20000))
confirm._resample_stability(res, null, 'greater', 0.05)  # spikes ~400MB; pure path would not
findings.suggested_fix: Sort the null once and use `np.searchsorted` to get per-resample extreme counts in O((R+N)logN) time and O(R+N) memory, avoiding the R×N mask entirely. For 'greater': `cnt = len(null) - np.searchsorted(null_sorted, res_arr, side='left')`; for 'less': `cnt = np.searchsorted(null_sorted, res_arr, side='right')`. This yields the identical integer extreme-counts (so the `(cnt+1)/(n+1) <= alpha` test is byte-identical) while removing the quadratic allocation. If keeping the broadcast form, at minimum chunk the resamples (process `res` in fixed-size blocks) so peak memory is bounded by block_size×N, and document the memory characteristic in the docstring.

findings.title: corpus_fingerprint materializes the full unit/relation payload before the 'streaming' hash, defeating the streaming optimization's intent
findings.description: `corpus_fingerprint` was changed (pass 3, #F-016) to stream JSON via `JSONEncoder.iterencode` 'rather than materialize one giant string' so a large corpus does not hold the full serialized payload in RAM at hash time. But the function first builds two full Python lists — `units` (a dict per unit with `_plain(u.attributes)` deep-canonicalized) and `relations` — and wraps them in a single `payload` dict, which is then handed whole to `iterencode`. For a large corpus this re-materializes the entire row set (plus a deep-canonicalized copy of every attribute mapping) in memory on top of the corpus itself, which is the dominant memory cost; the iterencode change only avoids the comparatively small extra copy of the final JSON *string*. The optimization's stated goal (bounded RAM at corpus scale) is therefore only partially met, and the docstring overstates the benefit.
findings.poc:
# For a corpus of N units each with a non-trivial attributes mapping, corpus_fingerprint
# allocates: (a) a list of N dicts, (b) a deep _plain() copy of every attributes map,
# (c) the relations list -- all live simultaneously before iterencode runs. Peak extra RAM
# is O(total corpus payload), not O(1) as the 'streaming' framing implies.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) soften the docstring to state honestly that only the serialized-string copy is avoided, the row dicts are still materialized; or (b) to genuinely bound memory, feed `iterencode` a generator-backed structure (e.g. hash units and relations incrementally — sort ids, then for each unit in sorted order update the hash with the canonical-JSON of just that row) so no full list/dict of the whole corpus is ever resident. Since this is a blueprint with no real corpus, (a) is the cheap, honest fix.

findings.title: Pipeline builds the corpus unit-id set twice on the same corpus (redundant O(U) pass)
findings.description: In `run_study`, line 435 builds `unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}` inside the DISCOVERY branch to validate split ids, and line 445 builds an identical `corpus_unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}` unconditionally for the feature-builder ghost-id check. When a study runs in DISCOVERY mode AND declares feature builders (the common case), the corpus units are iterated twice to construct the same set. Cheap to fix and trivially correct.
findings.poc:
# A DISCOVERY-mode study with feature_builders triggers two full passes over corpus.units
# building the identical {unit_id} set (pipeline.py:435 and :445).
findings.suggested_fix: Hoist a single `corpus_unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}` once, before the DISCOVERY split check, and reuse it for both the split-drift guard (line 436-441) and the feature-builder ghost-id guard (line 454-459). Drop the duplicate set comprehension.

findings.title: No test asserts the numpy resample-stability path's memory bound, and the pure↔numpy equivalence test uses only tiny inputs
findings.description: Test-quality gap tied to P-1: `test_resample_stability_numpy_path_matches_pure_python` validates numerical equivalence only on small arrays (null=40, res=25). There is no test exercising the path at any size that would surface the R×N allocation, and no test pinning the documented contract that the numpy path is 'purely a speed optimization, never a semantic change' against the memory dimension. As a result a future refactor to a chunked/searchsorted implementation (the P-1 fix) has no guardrail proving it preserves results at scale, and the current quadratic-memory behavior is invisible to the suite. This is the kind of coverage hole a discrimination auditor focused on correctness would miss because the existing test passes.
findings.poc:
# The only equivalence test (tests/test_confirm.py:191) uses null=40, res=25 -- far below any
# size where the R x N boolean mask is observable. No test covers large-R/large-N parity or any
# memory characteristic.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parametrized equivalence test over a moderately larger null/resample size (e.g. N=2000, R=2000) asserting numpy-path == pure-path, so any future memory-bounded rewrite (P-1) is held to byte-identical results. Optionally add a `tracemalloc`-based regression test that the stability computation's peak allocation scales sub-quadratically once P-1 lands.

findings.title: Unkeyed-trail warning and verify_trail=False fast path have no negative/behavioral test
findings.description: Test-quality gap. `run_study` emits a `warnings.warn(...)` when the provenance trail is unkeyed (secret=None, line 300-305) and supports `verify_trail=False` to skip the O(N) re-hash (line 702-710). I find no test asserting (a) that the unkeyed warning actually fires on the default path (so a future refactor silencing it would go unnoticed — the warning is the only signal an operator gets that the trail is forgery-vulnerable), nor (b) that `verify_trail=False` actually skips `trail.verify()` while still producing a valid result, nor (c) that passing both `secret` and `trail` raises the documented ValueError (line 291-292). These are error/edge paths whose absence means the safety affordances can silently regress.
findings.poc:
# grep of tests/ shows no assertion of the 'UNKEYED' warning, no test toggling verify_trail,
# and no test for the 'pass either a caller-owned trail OR a secret' ValueError guard.
findings.suggested_fix: Add focused tests: use `pytest.warns(UserWarning, match='UNKEYED')` around a default `run_study` call; assert `run_study(..., verify_trail=False)` returns a StudyResult and (via monkeypatch/spy on `ProvenanceTrail.verify`) that verify was not called on the success path while still being recoverable via `verify_records`; and `pytest.raises(ValueError)` when both `secret=` and `trail=` are passed.

content.text:
I completed the adversarial performance and test-quality audit of /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and returned findings via StructuredOutput.

Summary of what I found (5 findings, all medium/low — this codebase has been hardened over four passes and is genuinely mature):

Performance / complexity / memory:
- P-1 (medium) /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:217 — the numpy `_resample_stability` path allocates a full R×N boolean mask, an O(R·N) peak-memory blowup vs the O(N) pure-Python fallback it claims to merely "speed up". Fixable with `np.searchsorted` on the sorted null for byte-identical counts at O(R+N) memory.
- P-2 (low) /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py:53 — `corpus_fingerprint`'s "streaming" hash still materializes the full unit/relation payload (plus deep-canonicalized attribute copies) before `iterencode`, so the documented memory benefit is overstated.
- P-3 (low) /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:445 — the corpus unit-id set is built twice on the same corpus (lines 435 and 445) in the common DISCOVERY+feature-builders case.

Test-quality gaps (distinct from a discrimination auditor's scope):
- P-4 (medium) — the pure↔numpy resample-stability equivalence test uses only tiny inputs (null=40/res=25); no large-input parity guard, so a P-1 rewrite would be unprotected and the quadratic memory is invisible to the suite.
- P-5 (low) — no test covers the unkeyed-trail `warnings.warn`, the `verify_trail=False` fast path, or the `secret`+`trail` mutual-exclusion ValueError in `run_study` — safety affordances that can silently regress.

I deliberately kept scale findings to ones that are cheap to fix or genuinely risky, given this is a blueprint with no real corpus yet. The core hot paths (canonical hashing, content-addressed store, vector upsert batching, frozen-dict caching, provenance chaining, reasoning seam pooling) are already well-engineered with prior audit fixes addressing most obvious complexity/memory concerns.
